title: Coronavirus: Juliette Kayyem on Opening Up Policies
summary: Juliette Kayyem, Faculty Chair of the Belfer Center’s Security and Global Health Project, provides real-time perspective on opening up after the coronavirus crisis.
body: Juliette Kayyem, Faculty Chair of the Belfer Center’s Security and Global Health Project, has been providing real-time perspective on the coronavirus on Twitter. Read her recent comments below. The latest from Belfer See more Thread: "Opening up" is neither a singular policy nor a moment in time. As I've been reviewing "opening up" policies globally, it is clear we have a naming problem. It allows for these non-debates and silly editorials, as if there is a pro-isolation and anti-isolation crowd. 1/ — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 So, let's be clear. . . We will open up lest anyone doubt that those of us pushing for stay-at-home orders were loving this. Nope, it buys us time. That time hopefully is working. Critics clamoring to open are acting like they are first to think that isolation is temporary. 2/ — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 It is (say it again) the preparedness paradox, the tiresome refrain from those who say "look, it's not that bad (debatable), you folks all panicked, let's be free." That notion caricatures what social distancing was really about, creating camps where there are none. 3/ — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 "Opening up" is neither a singular policy nor a moment in time. Parks open in Germany allowing only two people together. New Zealand allows patio restaurants. Retail opens in Georgia with curbside pickup. Colorado experimenting with recreation. Michigan allowing golf. 4/ — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 University presidents, such as at Brown and Indiana, are mapping out strategies to return to school in September; controversial but worth thinking through. Employers distributing PPE. Vulnerable populations protected. It's the beginnings of our adaptive recovery stage. 5/ — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 These are often great experiments and while we need much more testing, we also have to come to the realization that we may not be able to wait for the perfect. We will accept losses that might have been avoidable. We have to admit that to ourselves and each other. 6/ — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 But there is responsible "opening up" and then there are tattoo parlors. They are not the same. So those "opening up" intellects, give us the details of how. I suspect they are exactly the same as those who pushed for isolation in the first place. Paradox? Exactly. 7/7 — Juliette Kayyem (@juliettekayyem) April 26, 2020 Recommended citation Kayyem, Juliette. “Coronavirus: Juliette Kayyem on Opening Up Policies.” Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School , April 27, 2020 Copy to Clipboard Coronavirus: Juliette Kayyem on Opening Up Policies View Citation Print Coronavirus Global Health Security and Global Health Recommended citation Kayyem, Juliette. “Coronavirus: Juliette Kayyem on Opening Up Policies.” Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School , April 27, 2020 Copy to Clipboard Author Juliette Kayyem Request Interview More From This Expert Securing the World’s Biggest Moments: Major Strategies in Megaevent Safety Planning Managing Supply And Value Chains In A New Era Of Global Trade: The Promise Of Federated Learning Up Next Photo Credit Toggle Associated Press - Daniel Kozin Blog Post H3N2 “Subclade K” Is Spreading. _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: Juliette Kayyem critiques the simplistic framing of 'opening up' policies during the coronavirus pandemic, arguing it is neither a single policy nor a single moment. She emphasizes that stay-at-home orders were a temporary measure to buy time, not a permanent preference, and warns against the 'preparedness paradox' where successful mitigation efforts are used to argue against their initial necessity. Kayyem highlights that reopening is a complex, adaptive recovery stage involving varied, localized experiments such as Germany's park rules, New Zealand's patio dining, and Georgia's retail curbside pickup, alongside university planning and workplace PPE distribution. She stresses the need for responsible, detailed reopening strategies that accept unavoidable losses while distinguishing between sensible measures and reckless openings, noting that the same experts advocating for isolation are often those best equipped to plan a safe reopening.
result.main_content_zh: 朱丽叶·凯耶姆对新冠疫情中“开放”政策的简单化框架提出批评，认为它既非单一政策，也非单一时刻。她强调，居家令是争取时间的临时措施，而非永久性偏好，并警告需警惕“准备悖论”，即成功的缓解措施反而被用来质疑其最初必要性。凯耶姆指出，重新开放是一个复杂的适应性恢复阶段，涉及多样化的本地化实验，例如德国的公园限流、新西兰的露天餐饮、佐治亚州的路边取货，以及大学的复课规划和工作场所的个人防护装备分发。她强调需要负责任、详细的重新开放策略，接受不可避免的损失，同时区分明智措施与鲁莽开放，并指出最初倡导隔离的专家往往正是规划安全重启的最佳人选。
